schools.summary: Pilate, wishing to save Jesus, orders the scourging (causative — he did not do it himself) hoping to satisfy the mob with a punishment short of death (Mt 27:26; Isa 53:5).
Augustine: Pilate scourged to cool the Jews to a milder mood; Calvin: a light chastisement to placate them, but overruled by the decree of Heaven.

The soldiers dress Jesus as a sham king — purple robe and a crown of thorns (Mt 27:29); Lightfoot: the thorns (the earth’s curse, Gen 3:18) show his kingdom is not of this world; Gill: thorns emblem of his people’s sins compassing him.
Calvin: done by Pilate’s authority to mark the Son of God with infamy, the soldiers’ insolence overruled by God.

Repeated mock homage (imperfects): the soldiers come bowing and saluting ‘Hail, King of the Jews!’ as if to Caesar, while striking him — a bitter parody of a king’s court, which the patient Christ bears in royal silence.
Pilate again declares ‘I find no fault in him’ (18:38) even as he produces the scourged Jesus, hoping the pitiable sight will move the mob — punishing one he repeatedly pronounces guiltless.
Cyril: let us not be ashamed of the Cross but glory in it — a stumbling-block to Jews, foolishness to Gentiles, but to us salvation.

Pilate brings out the scourged, thorn-crowned, purple-robed Jesus and cries ‘Behold the man!’ (Ecce Homo) — to move the mob to pity or contempt; Christ bears the mockery with kingly dignity (Heb 12:2).

The sight inflames rather than softens the rulers — ‘Crucify him, crucify him’ (urgent imperative, Mt 27:22); Pilate’s ‘Take ye him’ is an ironic refusal, not permission — he will not be their executioner, ‘I find no fault in him.’
Augustine: their envy rises rather than subsides at his disgrace; Chrysostom: Pilate speaks as one abhorring the deed they demand.
Failing with sedition (Lk 23:2), the rulers name the real ground — blasphemy (Lev 24:16): ‘he made himself the Son of God,’ the long-standing charge (5:18) on which they condemned him (Mk 14:61-64).
Calvin: they pretend to act righteously, not from hatred, stung by Pilate’s reproof — yet the charge is in truth the confession of who he is.

themes: ‘he made himself the Son of God’

schools.summary: The charge that Jesus ‘made himself the Son of God’ deepens Pilate’s fear — superstitious dread (with his wife’s warning, Mt 27:19) and wonder who Jesus really is; the capital charge from their law changes his footing.
Calvin: he dreaded blame from a tumult, or more likely his mind was moved by religion at the name ‘Son of God’; Wesley: already afraid of shedding innocent blood, now the more.

themes: fear at the claim of deity
the title that terrifies the heathen

schools.summary: ‘Whence art thou?’ asks not his country (a Galilean, Lk 23:6) but his nature — man or divine; the alarmed Pilate gets no answer, Jesus having already spoken enough (18:36-37) to one who would not act.
Augustine: the silence (before high priest, Herod, Pilate) fulfils Isa 53:7, ‘as a sheep dumb before her shearers’; Chrysostom: Pilate, who should have rescued him, is owed no more.

themes: the Lamb dumb before his shearers

schools.summary: Wounded pride: ‘To me dost thou not speak?’ — Pilate, resenting the silence as contempt, boasts of his twofold ‘authority’ (exousia) to crucify or release, wavering between fearing and bullying Jesus.
Calvin: his sudden dread (v.8) was transitory and rootless — he now breaks into haughty contempt of God, as irreligious men shake off conviction once it passes.

themes: ‘knowest thou not that I have power?’

schools.summary: Pilate’s authority is no possession of his own but ‘given from above’ (anōthen, God; Rom 13:1) — the doctrine of ordained government; the ‘greater sin’ is that of the one who delivered him (Caiaphas/the Jews/Judas), whose malice outweighs Pilate’s ignorance.
Calvin: God’s overruling providence (Pilate does no more than God permits) joined to real human guilt — permission does not excuse the actors.

debates.question: Who is ‘he that delivered me’ with the greater sin?

themes: Christ the Judge of his judges

schools.summary: Moved by Christ’s words, Pilate ‘kept seeking’ to release him (one of five attempts, Lk 23), but the threat ‘thou art not Caesar’s friend’ — a danger of denunciation to a jealous emperor — breaks his resolve.
Augustine: the Jews alarm him more by Caesar than by their law; Calvin: Pilate, irresolute and ruled by ambition rather than justice, yields to the fear of man.
Pilate takes his seat on the judgment platform (bēma) at ‘the Pavement’ (Lithostrōton, coloured stones; Esther 1:6), Hebrew ‘Gabbatha’ (the high place); the examination over, he hesitates no longer.
Calvin: Pilate ‘like a stage-player acting two characters’ ascends to sentence Christ solemnly yet openly declares he does so reluctantly.

themes: the forms of justice in injustice

schools.summary: ‘Preparation of the passover’ = Friday of passover week (Mk 15:42); John’s ‘sixth hour’ vs Mark’s ‘third’ (15:25) reconciled by the looseness of ancient time-reckoning; ‘Behold your King!’ is Pilate’s bitter irony.
Calvin: acknowledges the apparent difference among the Evangelists on the hour and offers the usual reconciliation.

themes: the Lamb in the hour of sacrifice

schools.summary: The frenzied ‘Away with him’ (aron, aron = kill him; Isa 57:1); Pilate’s taunt ‘Shall I crucify your King?’; and the climactic apostasy: ‘We have no king but Caesar.’
Calvin: ‘shocking madness’ — the priests reject Christ, in whom all the promises were contained, depriving themselves; Cyril: awe that the redeemed people shout against their Redeemer.
Pilate ‘delivered’ (paredōken) Jesus to their will for crucifixion (carried out by the soldiers), having washed his hands (Mt 27:24) — a hand-washing that cannot cleanse the guilt he disowns.
Henry: Pilate’s convictions yielded and his corruptions prevailed, the fear of man outweighing the fear of God; behind it, the Son is ‘delivered up’ by the Father (Rom 8:32).
Christ bears his own cross (the transverse beam) as condemned men did, alone until Simon was compelled (Mt 27:32), out to ‘Golgotha,’ the place of a skull (Calvary).

Calvin: led ‘out of the city’ and hanged to prove he is the sin-offering, suffering ‘without the gate’ as the sacrifices were burned outside the camp (Heb 13:12; Num 15:36); Augustine ponders the Lord bearing his own cross.
The soldiers crucify Jesus at Golgotha with two robbers (lēistai, not thieves; Mt 27:38; perhaps Barabbas’ gang), one on each side, ‘Jesus in the midst’ — the central place of the chief malefactor.
Calvin: hanged between two robbers as if the most detestable of all — yet fulfilling Scripture, ‘numbered with the transgressors’ (Isa 53:12), the sinless One in the sinner’s place.

themes: two robbers, Jesus in the midst

schools.summary: Pilate himself (John alone says so) wrote the ‘title’ (titlos, the technical Latin term) naming the charge — ‘JESUS OF NAZARETH THE KING OF THE JEWS’ (Mt 27:37; Mk 15:26).

Chrysostom: like letters on a victory-trophy, distinguishing Christ from the thieves and exposing the Jews’ malice against their King; Calvin: the ‘extraordinary’ thing — a title that proclaims a truth, not a crime.

The cross being near the city, ‘many of the Jews’ read the title, written in Hebrew, Greek, and Latin (Lk 23:38) — the tongues of religion, culture, and empire; a detail peculiar to John’s exact knowledge.

The chief priests, alarmed that ‘King of the Jews’ honours Christ and shames the nation, ask Pilate to change it to a mere claim (‘he said, I am King’) — ‘right in their fear’ that the mockery was on them.
Calvin: stung by the rebuke, they would shift the blame to Christ alone, yet betray their hatred of a truth even a spark of which they cannot endure.

themes: ‘Write not, The King of the Jews’

schools.summary: Pilate’s curt, final ‘What I have written I have written’ (perfect tenses, permanence) — a sudden stubbornness, partly impatience, partly Roman law forbidding alteration of a pronounced sentence.
Calvin: his firmness is the providence of God, holding him unmoved against the priests’ pressure, so the testimony to Christ’s kingship stands beyond recall.

themes: ‘What I have written I have written’

schools.summary: The four soldiers (a quaternion, Acts 12:4) divide the outer garments and treat apart the ‘coat without seam, woven from the top’ — the criminal’s clothes their perquisite; John’s account fullest.
Augustine and the fathers find in the seamless coat an emblem (the unity of the church / Christ’s whole righteousness); Calvin notes the indignity — soldiers gambling as the Saviour dies.

The soldiers’ choice — divide the garments but cast lots for the seamless coat — fulfils Ps 22:18 exactly (the two clauses distinguished in the event); Wesley: David’s life gave no parallel, so it is bare prophecy.
Augustine dwells on the precise fulfilment; Cyril appeals to it as an exact sign of the foretold Passion.

The faithful women (three or four named, incl. Mary of Cleophas = wife of Alphaeus, Mt 27:56) stand near the cross — a vivid contrast to the gambling soldiers; foremost his mother, the sword piercing her soul (Lk 2:35).
Calvin: even in agony Christ performs the duty of a son; the women’s devotion, braving shame when the apostles fled, is a pattern of cleaving love.
‘Woman’ is a title of honour, not disrespect (2:4); ‘Behold thy son’ means John, who will be a son to her — the dying Saviour providing for his mother, Joseph being dead.
Calvin: ‘I put this man in my room to perform my office’ — Christ, bearing the world’s sin, yet honours his mother to the last, fulfilling the fifth commandment.
The double committal — Mary given a son, John given a mother — and the disciple’s prompt obedience: ‘from that hour’ he took her into his own home and care.

themes: love in the family of Christ

schools.summary: Christ, fully conscious that all was ‘finished’ (tetelestai; 13:1), cries ‘I thirst’ — a real thirst (the proof of his true humanity) that also fulfils Scripture (Ps 69:21).
Augustine: more than bodily craving — ‘give me yourselves,’ a thirst for the salvation of those who gave him vinegar; the Giver of living water thirsts that his people might never thirst.

The ‘vinegar’ (oxous, the soldiers’ common sour wine) — distinct from the drugged wine Jesus refused (Mk 15:23; Mt 27:34) — is raised on a sponge fixed to hyssop (which grows shrub-like there) to his mouth, fulfilling Ps 69:21.

themes: refused the drugged, took the plain

schools.summary: ‘It is finished’ (tetelestai) — a cry of victory, not defeat (cf. 16:33): the work the Father gave (17:4), the demands of justice satisfied, the way to the holiest opened; he ‘gave up his spirit’ voluntarily (10:18).
Calvin/Barnes: the work long promised and expected is done, man redeemed — the price paid in full; the believer’s whole hope rests on the finished work.

To avoid bodies remaining over the sabbath (Deut 21:22-23), an ‘high day’ (sabbath + passover + firstfruits), the rulers ask Pilate for the crurifragium (breaking the legs) to hasten death and removal.
Chrysostom: they strain at a gnat (the day) having swallowed the camel (his murder); Theophylact: their own devices fulfil prophecy; Calvin: the narrative holds a great mystery.
The same quaternion (v.23), come forward to finish, break the legs of the two robbers ‘crucified with him’ (synstaurōthentos; cf. Gal 2:19) to hasten death before the sabbath.
Origen: reads the scene within the passover typology that fills the chapter — the law of the true Paschal Lamb working out.

themes: the soldiers break the robbers’ legs

schools.summary: Coming to Jesus, the soldiers find him ‘already dead’ (his death hastened by his agony and atoning suffering) and so do not break his legs — the crurifragium being unnecessary.
Calvin: ‘a very extraordinary work of the providence of God’ — ordering even the soldiers’ omission so not a bone of the Lamb should be broken (v.36).
The spear-thrust and the flow of ‘blood and water’ prove Jesus truly dead (a physiological sign), recorded by the eyewitness and fulfilling Scripture (v.37; Zech 12:10).
Calvin and the tradition: the blood and water emblem the twofold grace from Christ’s death — blood of atonement, water of cleansing (the double cure; cf. 1 John 5:6); the fountain opened (Zech 13:1).

John, the modest eyewitness (speaking in the third person, 1:7), solemnly swears to the piercing and the blood and water — a personal witness disproving the Docetic denial of Christ’s real body — ‘that ye might believe.’
The unbroken bones fulfil the law of the paschal lamb (Exod 12:46; Num 9:12; cf. Ps 34:20) — Christ the true Lamb (1 Cor 5:7), kept whole in death as the type required.
Calvin: Moses assumes the lamb a figure of the one true sacrifice; the providence of v.33 was the fulfilment of the passover type.

themes: ‘a bone of him shall not be broken’

schools.summary: The spear-thrust fulfils Zech 12:10 (‘pierced,’ exekentēsan; cf. Rev 1:7) — so many Scriptures fulfilled without the actors’ design; the prophecy’s ‘me’ is the Lord himself.
Calvin: it shows Christ is the God who complained by Zechariah of being pierced; Wesley: men shall look with penitence now or terror at his coming (Rev 1:7).

themes: ‘they shall look on him whom they pierced’
the deity of the pierced One

schools.summary: Joseph, a disciple ‘secretly for fear of the Jews’ (cf. 12:42-43), now grows bold when the apostles have fled, begging Pilate for the body (Mt 27:57) — to his credit, an open stand at last.
Calvin: the body, else exposed to the soldiers, is rescued for honourable burial (cf. Isa 53:9); Wesley: ‘Joseph no longer afraid, Nicodemus no longer ashamed.’

themes: with the rich in his death (Isa 53:9)

schools.summary: Nicodemus, who first came ‘by night’ (3:1-2; 7:50), now takes an open stand, bringing a princely hundred pounds of myrrh and aloes to embalm the body — a king’s burial.
Cyril: the garden of the tomb echoes the Canticles (‘I am come into my garden’) where the true Vine was planted to root out the curse.
Joseph and Nicodemus wind the body in linen strips (othoniois) with the spices, ‘as the manner of the Jews is to bury’ (entaphiazein; cf. 11:44; Acts 5:6) — an honourable, customary preparation.
Calvin: God ordered an honourable burial after the cross’s ignominy, as a preparation for the glory of the resurrection — the cost not superfluous but purposeful.
A garden near the crucifixion held a ‘new’ (kainon) rock-tomb, Joseph’s own (Mt 27:60), ‘never man yet laid’ — its nearness suiting the haste, its newness guarding the proof of the resurrection.
Calvin: not accident but God’s providence; Cyril: the garden echoes Eden and the Canticles — the curse begun in a garden undone where the true Vine is laid.
The lateness of the preparation day (the sabbath drawing on) and the nearness of the new tomb pressed a hasty burial there — the convenient place for the urgent hour.
Calvin: the burial-narrative is told to show it was no accident but God’s providence — Christ truly dead and buried, with witnesses to the place, setting the stage for the resurrection.
